topic: How accurate labour cost data changed the way one owner made pricing decisions
format: Open with a data point about Oman businesses and explain the root cause behind it
post:
67% of SMEs in Oman price their services without knowing their real labour cost per hour.

Not because they don't care. Because the data is buried in spreadsheets, WhatsApp threads, and payroll files that never talk to each other.

One business owner in Muscat learned this the hard way.

He'd been quoting projects the same way for three years — materials cost, rough time estimate, add a margin. It felt like pricing. It wasn't.

When he connected his WPS payroll Oman data with actual hours worked per job, the number was uncomfortable.

His margin on mid-size contracts wasn't 22%. It was 6%.

He wasn't growing. He was subsidising his clients.

Once his payroll, workforce hours, and project costs were in one system — HR software Oman built for exactly this — he repriced within a week.

Three months later, margin was back above 19%. Same team. Same clients. Just accurate numbers.

The software didn't fix his business. Knowing the truth did.

If you're still estimating labour costs from memory or last month's payroll summary, you're pricing blind.

What would change if you knew your real cost per employee, per project, per month?
